AI Strategy Experience Design Group + Personal Extension

Putumayo
World Music.

From Western curation to participatory cultural exchange — redesigning how a world music brand creates authentic cultural value through immersive experience design and an AI-powered curation strategy.

RoleExperience Strategist
Year2026
TypeGroup Project + Personal Extension
Score83/100
01 — Overview
Context &
Problem

Putumayo built its identity on making the world feel smaller — bright, illustrated CDs of world music, stacked next to your coffee at Starbucks. It worked. For a while. Then streaming arrived, the CDs disappeared, and so did the cultural depth that made Putumayo worth caring about.

But the problem runs deeper than format. Putumayo's curation has always been shaped by a Western editorial lens — Asia reduced to Middle Eastern sounds, Africa collapsed into a single category despite hundreds of distinct cultures. Users feel the rhythm but can't place it culturally.

BrandPutumayo World Music — Record label & streaming platform
ProblemWestern editorial bias, passive consumption, no cultural context
GoalMove from passive listening to participatory cultural exchange
ContextWOMAD Festival 2026, Wiltshire, UK — immersive pop-up experience
Methods1:1 interviews · Persona · CJM · Pine & Gilmore mapping · Prioritisation matrix
02 — Research
What Users
Actually Said
Fernando · Mexico · M · 24
"I was influenced a lot from my parent's music taste. That's how I experienced world music for the first time."
"Putumayo's album design presented our culture mood well."
Vivian · China · W · 22
"I don't know why they put category name as Asia — they only have Middle-East Asia music."
"Peking Opera is our culture music, but modern Chinese pop mixed with Western style."
Moses · Kenya · M · 50
"Africa has very diverse tribes, so it's difficult to identify as 'African music'."
"Our music is completed not only with rhythm and instruments, but also dance and dress."
Insight 01
Cultural Bias in Curation
Asia is largely absent beyond Middle Eastern music. Africa's vast diversity of tribes, instruments, and rhythms is collapsed into a single category. Users experience music without cultural context.
Insight 02
Passive Consumption — No Depth
The platform offers no mechanism for users to understand why a track belongs to a culture. Low retention, low emotional connection, no advocacy behaviour.
Insight 03
Identity & Heritage Matter
Users from underrepresented cultures feel unseen or misrepresented. Cultural music is deeply tied to family, identity, and memory — not just passive listening.
03 — Define
Persona &
Customer Journey

A single primary persona was developed to maintain clarity within the sprint constraints — Janet Alves, 37, a London-based doctor who immigrated from another country and raises a young child in the UK.

Who She Is
Janet Alves, 37 — London
Values cultural identity preservation and wants her child to feel proud of their heritage while also exploring global cultures. Regularly attends family-friendly festivals and workshops.
What She Needs
Authentic cultural representation
A safe, age-appropriate cultural experience. Authentic and respectful representation of different cultures. Interactive music experience that goes beyond passive listening.
Her Challenge
Difficulty finding trustworthy experiences
Many platforms provide passive listening but lack interaction. Uncertainty about whether cultural activities are age-appropriate. Hard to find authentic, trustworthy cultural content.
CJM Key Finding

The Customer Journey Map revealed a critical drop-off: Janet starts curious, but becomes emotionally disengaged during the Use and Retention stages. There's no cultural context, no interactivity, and nothing to take away. High initial curiosity → rapid emotional disengagement → no advocacy.

04 — Hypothesis
Strategic Direction
If we...
Shift Putumayo from a centralised curatorial authority to a participatory model — where regional artists, local record labels, and audiences co-create the experience —
Then users will develop a deeper cultural connection and perceive the brand as trustworthy and authentic.
Principle 01
Transparent
Show why music is categorised the way it is. Transparent curation notes and cultural context at every touchpoint.
Principle 02
Trustworthy
Co-curate with regional partners, not just Western editors. Arnstein's Ladder — toward Citizen Control in the playlisting process.
Principle 03
Participatory
Users contribute to the next playlist, not just consume. From passive listener to active co-creator of cultural value.
05 — Solution
WOMAD Pop-up 2026
3-Zone Experience

A three-zone cultural journey at WOMAD Festival, Wiltshire — designed to move visitors from passive absorption to active co-creation, leaving with genuine cultural understanding and a contribution to Putumayo's next playlist.

Zone 1
Music Discovery
Six continent-based jukeboxes co-curated with regional record labels. Transparent curation notes and cultural video content extend listening into cultural understanding. Visitors select postcards with QR codes — their choice becomes soft data for the next Putumayo playlist.
→ Increased perceived authenticity and brand trust
Educational
Zone 2
Motion Communication
Motion-capture visuals respond to dance in real time — abstract shapes instead of human representations for full inclusivity. Sound becomes shared sensory interaction at the emotional and sensory peak of the experience. Culture felt through the body, not just the ear.
→ Peak emotional engagement, embodied cultural participation
Escapist
Zone 3
Creative Workshop
Participants colour illustrated postcards and "send" them via a postbox scanner. Their preference data informs future playlist curation. Staff explain data usage clearly and give visitors the choice — consent-based participation. A guided reflection moment for meaning-making.
→ User becomes co-creator, reciprocal artist-audience relationship
Creative
Pop-up Floorplan
WOMAD Festival 2026 · Wiltshire, UK
06 — Personal Extension
AI Curating
Service Concept

The pop-up generates real behavioural data — which tracks people gravitated toward, which postcards they chose, how they moved in Zone 2. This is seed data for something bigger: a bias-aware AI curation engine.

Feature 01
Cultural Context Engine
Tags each track by instrument, rhythm origin, and cultural tradition — not just geography. Granular, accurate categorisation replacing broad generalisations like "African music".
Feature 02
Bias Detection Layer
Flags when a regional category is under-represented or over-generalised. Structurally fairer curation at scale — addressing the Asia/Africa blind spots identified in research.
Feature 03
Explainable Curation
Every recommendation includes why it was suggested and what culture it comes from. Users leave knowing more than when they arrived — music you feel, culture you understand.
Product Vision
"Music you feel. Culture you understand."
07 — Impact
How I Would
Measure Success
Shift in perceived brand authenticity — pre/post experience survey at WOMAD
Postcard scan rate as a proxy for co-creation engagement and consent-based data participation
Playlist diversity index: regional distribution of user-selected tracks across 6 continents
Return engagement rate with Putumayo's streaming service post-festival
Bias score improvement across AI-curated playlists over time — representation tracking
This project was conducted within an academic group sprint, scoring 83/100. Success metrics were defined conceptually to demonstrate measurement readiness and product thinking.
08 — Reflection
What I Learned
83
Academic score
out of 100
"Designing for cultural experiences isn't just about aesthetics — it's about power."

Who gets to decide what "world music" sounds like? Whose culture gets reduced to a mood, and whose gets a detailed liner note? This project pushed me to apply UX frameworks not just to improve usability, but to challenge the ethical structure of a product itself.

The AI product concept isn't just an academic exercise. As someone interested in AI strategy, I want to build systems that make algorithmic curation more transparent and culturally accountable.

◈
Cultural bias in product is invisible to those it doesn't affect
Surface it through research, not assumption. The 1:1 interviews were the most important part of the process.
◎
Co-creation is most powerful when the output is real
Participants here shaped an actual future playlist — not a fictional exercise. That made the engagement genuine.
◇
Ethical design isn't a constraint — it was the experience
Moving from Arnstein's lower rungs toward Citizen Control became the design brief itself, not just a consideration.
○
Physical data can seed AI in ways digital data can't
Postcard selections and motion data carry embodied preference signals that passive streaming data misses entirely.
